matchups.tacticalAdvantage: Duke enters as the overall No. 1 seed after a 32-2 season. Despite late-season injuries to Caleb Foster, the talent gap anchored by Cameron Boozer is too wide for Siena to overcome. This aligns with historical data, where 1-seeds win 99.3% of the time, easily supporting Duke's -27.5 point betting spread.

Historically, 8-vs-9 matchups are a near coin flip (9-seeds win 48.7%). Ohio State relies heavily on senior guard Bruce Thornton (124.3 AdjO), but TCU brings an elite defense (97.8 AdjD) and a 75% cover rate as an underdog. The tight -2.5 spread in Ohio State's favor makes TCU an appealing, albeit coin-flip, upset pick.

While 12-seeds historically upset 5-seeds 35.3% of the time, this specific matchup is a trap. Northern Iowa is utterly atrocious on the offensive glass (356th nationally). St. John's ranks 28th nationally in offensive rebounds and will overpower them, validating their -10.5 point spread.

Kansas features potential top NBA pick Darryn Peterson. CBU relies on Dominique Daniels but is making its first NCAA tournament appearance. Historically, 4-seeds win 79.5% of these matchups, and Kansas fits the mold as a comfortable -14.5 point favorite.

Louisville is missing primary playmaker Mikel Brown Jr. South Florida plays at a blistering pace and ranks second nationally in rebounds (43 per game). Historically, 11-seeds only win 37.8% of the time, which tempers absolute certainty, but the stylistic matchup and tight -6.5 spread makes USF highly live.

Michigan State extended its Big Ten record to 28 consecutive NCAA Tournament appearances. They share the ball exceptionally well and face a Summit League champion in NDSU. 3-seeds historically win 85.3% of the time, and the Spartans' -16.5 spread reflects that dominance.

UCF features an explosive offense averaging 81 points per game and plays a tough style. UCLA relies heavily on Tyler Bilodeau but has battled uneven performances. 10-seeds historically win about 40% of the time, making this a risky upset, but UCF's relentless pace makes them an intriguing value play against a -5.5 spread.

Furman is a dangerous mid-major, but UConn under Dan Hurley is a dynastic mastermind with too much depth. 2-seeds boast a 93.6% historical win rate, and the Huskies command a massive -19.5 point spread.

Arizona opened the season with 23 straight wins and is the model's projected national champion, featuring top-five ranks in both offensive and defensive efficiency. Historical 1-seed dominance (99.3%) secures this -29.5 spread.

Historical 8-vs-9 matchups are a toss-up, but Utah State possesses a highly balanced offensive attack against a Villanova squad overly reliant on three-point variance and missing Matt Hodge. Oddsmakers agree, installing the 9-seed Aggies as -1.5 point favorites.

High Point enters on a 14-game winning streak and thrives on offensive execution, while Wisconsin ranks 177th in opponent effective FG%. Historically, 12-seeds win 35.3% of the time, so absolute conviction is dangerous, but High Point's momentum creates a strong statistical upset opportunity.

Arkansas won the SEC Tournament, but sharp money heavily favors Hawai'i to cover the +15.5 spread. 13-seeds historically win only ~20% of the time, so predicting an outright upset is highly speculative, but Hawai'i can exploit Arkansas's defensive lapses to keep it close.

Texas advanced out of the First Four with momentum. BYU attempts to overcome the loss of key rotation piece Richie Saunders. While 11-seeds historically win 37.8% of the time, BYU's injuries make this a prime spot for Texas to overperform.

Gonzaga secured a No. 3 seed and boasts elite efficiency. While considered less dominant than classic Bulldogs teams, historical 3-seed dominance (85.3%) and a -18.5 point spread support them comfortably advancing over Kennesaw State.

Missouri dropped to the 10-line after struggling down the stretch, drawing a battle-tested ACC opponent. Miami holds the edge in analytics (No. 31 KenPom vs No. 52), aligning with the historical 60% win rate for 7-seeds.
Purdue enters at 27-8 after winning the Big Ten Tournament. 2-seeds boast a 93.6% historical win rate against 15-seeds, making Purdue's No. 8 KenPom rating an overwhelming advantage.
Michigan boasts the country's most efficient defense (No. 1 AdjD), anchored by 7-foot-7 rim protector Aday Mara. This aligns perfectly with the 99.3% historical win rate of 1-seeds.

8-vs-9 matchups are historical 50/50 toss-ups, but Georgia leads the nation in fast-break points and boasts an elite +3.09 turnover margin. Sharp money backs Georgia to exploit Saint Louis's 230th rank in offensive turnovers.

Texas Tech lost star forward JT Toppin, forcing them into a predictable offense. Akron ranks in the top fifteen nationally in shooting percentage. While 12-seeds historically win only 35.3% of the time, Toppin's absence provides Akron a strong opening.

Alabama lost guard Aden Holloway, compromising perimeter spacing. Hofstra plays at a glacial pace to exploit Alabama's poor defensive rebounding. However, 13-seeds historically win just over 20% of the time, so while the stylistic matchup screams upset, history demands tempered expectations.

Miami (OH) built a 31-1 record on a weak schedule. While 11-seeds are trendy upset picks (winning 37.8% historically), Tennessee is battle-tested, healthy, and will overwhelm Miami with SEC-tier speed and a 44.7% offensive rebounding rate.
Virginia secured a No. 3 seed with their trademark pack-line defense and will suffocate the Horizon League champion. 3-seeds historically win 85.3% of matchups, supporting Virginia's -17.5 spread.

Kentucky struggles to score in half-court sets and is missing point guard Jaland Lowe. 10-seeds historically win 39.7% of the time, making this generally risky, but Kentucky being favored by only -3.5 points signals extreme vulnerability against Santa Clara's outside shooting.
Iowa State relies on interior dominance and boasts incredible defensive metrics. The historical 93.6% win rate for 2-seeds against 15-seeds easily validates the Cyclones' -23.5 point spread.
Florida is the defending national champion and won the SEC regular-season title. The massive talent gap perfectly aligns with the 99.3% historical win rate of 1-seeds.

8-vs-9 matchups are notoriously close (9-seeds win 48.7%). Iowa brings strong March experience and opened as a slight -1.5 point favorite, heavily influenced by Clemson missing key rebounder Carter Welling to a torn ACL.

Vanderbilt is severely underseeded (12th in KenPom) and shoots 79% from the free-throw line. While 12-seeds secure upsets roughly 35% of the time, Vanderbilt's physical forwards are perfectly equipped to exploit McNeese's poor defensive rebounding, avoiding the trap.

Nebraska heavily regressed late in the season, and Troy limits shot efficiency at the basket. However, historically, 13-seeds only win 20.5% of First Round matchups. While Troy is highly live to cover the 13.5-point spread, predicting an outright upset here requires tempering expectations given historical precedent.

UNC lost interior enforcer Caleb Wilson. VCU's relentless ball pressure will disrupt half-court sets. Historically, 11-seeds win 37.8% of the time, but Oddsmakers see the vulnerability, opening UNC as a minuscule -1.5 point favorite.
Illinois possesses the country's most productive attack. Penn arrives hot, but faces a massive talent deficit. 3-seeds win 85.3% of the time, safely validating Illinois's -20.5 point spread.
A heavy stylistic clash: Saint Mary's dictates a slow slog, while Texas A&M pushes a fast track meet. 7-seeds win roughly 60% of the time, aligning with Saint Mary's narrow -1.5 point spread.
Houston was placed in the South Region, setting up a highly motivated run. With 2-seeds winning 93.6% of the time, Houston's No. 5 KenPom ranking easily supports their -19.5 spread.
